path.title: What is Python?
path.content:
Introduction to Python - Part 1

Welcome to Python Programming!

🐍 What is Python?
Python is a simple, powerful programming language used for:
• Web development
• Data science
• Artificial intelligence
• Automation
• Game development

💻 Why Python?
✓ Easy to learn - Simple, readable syntax
✓ Popular - Used by millions of developers
✓ Versatile - Works for many projects
✓ In-demand - Great career opportunities

🎯 Our Goal:
Learn Python basics and become a confident programmer!

examples.question: What is Python used for?
examples.solution:
Python is used for:
• Web development (Django, Flask)
• Data science (Pandas, NumPy)
• AI/Machine Learning (TensorFlow)
• Automation scripts
• Game development

examples.question: Why should I learn Python?
examples.solution:
Python is:
✓ Beginner-friendly
✓ Highly in-demand in tech jobs
✓ Used by major companies (Google, Netflix, Spotify)
✓ Great for building real projects
✓ Has huge community support

path.title: Your First Program
path.content:
Introduction to Python - Part 2

Let's write our first Python program!

📝 Hello World:
print("Hello, World!")

Output:
Hello, World!

🎯 What happened?
• print() is a function
• It displays text to the screen
• The text goes inside quotes

💡 Remember:
• Python reads top to bottom
• Indentation matters in Python
• Be careful with spelling!

examples.question: How do I display text in Python?
examples.solution:
Use the print() function:
print("Your text here")

Example:
print("Welcome to Python!")

Output:
Welcome to Python!

questions.question: What does print() do in Python?
Which is a valid Python print statement?

What will this code output?
print("Python")
What company did NOT use Python?
What should you use to display text?
What do quotes do in print?

path.title: Data Types - Numbers
path.content:
Data Types in Python - Part 1

What are Data Types?
Data types define what kind of data a variable holds.

🔢 Numbers in Python:

Integers (whole numbers):
age = 25
students = 100

Floats (decimal numbers):
price = 19.99
temperature = 98.6

📝 Examples:
print(5 + 3)        # Output: 8
print(10 - 2)       # Output: 8
print(4 * 5)        # Output: 20
print(20 / 4)       # Output: 5.0
print(10 % 3)       # Output: 1 (remainder)
print(2 ** 3)       # Output: 8 (power)

💡 Operations:
+ Addition
- Subtraction
* Multiplication
/ Division
% Modulo (remainder)
** Power/Exponent

examples.question: What's the difference between 5 and 5.0?
examples.solution:
5 is an integer (whole number)
5.0 is a float (decimal number)

Python treats them differently:
type(5) → int
type(5.0) → float

examples.question: What does 10 % 3 equal?
examples.solution:
10 % 3 = 1

The % is modulo (remainder)
10 divided by 3 = 3 remainder 1
So the answer is 1

path.title: 🎯 Practice: Number Operations
path.problem: What is the output of: print(15 + 6)
path.explanation:
To solve: print(15 + 6)
1. Python adds 15 + 6 = 21
2. print() displays: 21

questions.question: What symbol is used for multiplication?

path.title: Data Types - Strings
path.content:
Data Types in Python - Part 2

What are Strings?
Strings are text - any characters inside quotes!

📝 Creating Strings:
name = "Alice"
message = 'Hello World'
sentence = "Python is awesome!"

🎯 Single vs Double Quotes:
Both work the same!
"Hello" = 'Hello'

✨ String Operations:

Concatenation (joining):
first = "John"
last = "Doe"
full_name = first + " " + last
print(full_name)  # Output: John Doe

Repetition (repeating):
print("Ha" * 3)  # Output: HaHaHa

Length (counting characters):
text = "Python"
len(text)  # Output: 6

💡 Tips:
• Use + to combine strings
• Use * to repeat strings
• Use len() to count characters

examples.question: How do I combine two strings?
examples.solution:
Use the + operator:
first = "Hello"
second = "World"
result = first + " " + second
print(result)  # Output: Hello World

examples.question: How many characters are in 'Python'?
examples.solution:
Use len():
print(len("Python"))  # Output: 6

P-y-t-h-o-n = 6 characters

path.title: 🎯 Practice: String Operations
path.problem: What is the output of: print("Code" + "r")
path.explanation:
To solve: print("Code" + "r")
1. Python joins "Code" + "r" = "Coder"
2. print() displays: Coder

questions.question: Can you use single AND double quotes?

path.title: Data Types - Booleans
path.content:
Data Types in Python - Part 3

What are Booleans?
Booleans are True or False values!

✓ Boolean Values:
True
False

📝 Examples:
is_student = True
has_license = False
print(is_student)  # Output: True

🎯 Comparisons (return True/False):
5 > 3        # True
10 < 5       # False
7 == 7       # True (equals)
5 != 3       # True (not equals)
10 >= 10     # True

💡 Comparison Operators:
== Equal to
!= Not equal to
> Greater than
< Less than
>= Greater or equal
<= Less or equal

🔗 Logical Operators:
and - Both must be True
or - One must be True
not - Reverses True/False

Example:
age = 25
age > 18 and age < 65  # True

examples.question: What does 5 == 5 return?
examples.solution:
True

== is the equality operator
5 equals 5, so the answer is True

examples.question: What is True or False?
examples.solution:
True

With 'or', if ONE is True, the result is True
So: True or False = True

path.title: 🎯 Practice: Boolean Logic
path.explanation:
To solve: 10 > 5 and 3 < 10
1. 10 > 5 is True
2. 3 < 10 is True
3. True and True = True

path.title: Variables & Assignment
path.content:
Working with Variables

What are Variables?
Variables store data that you can use and change!

📝 Creating Variables:
name = "Alice"
age = 25
gpa = 3.8
is_student = True

🎯 Naming Variables:
✓ Good names: student_name, user_age, is_valid
✗ Bad names: x, a, data, stuff

💡 Rules:
• Start with a letter or underscore
• Use only letters, numbers, underscores
• No spaces in names
• Python is case-sensitive (age ≠ Age)

📝 Using Variables:
first_name = "John"
last_name = "Smith"
full_name = first_name + " " + last_name
print(full_name)  # Output: John Smith

print(type(first_name))  # Output: <class 'str'>
print(type(25))          # Output: <class 'int'>

examples.question: How do I create a variable?
examples.solution:
Use = to assign:
variable_name = value

Examples:
user = "Bob"
score = 95
is_active = True

examples.question: What's the best way to name variables?
examples.solution:
Use descriptive names that tell you what the variable stores:
✓ student_name
✓ user_age
✓ is_verified
✗ x
✗ data
✗ stuff

path.title: 🎯 Practice: Variables
path.problem:
What is the output?
name = "Python"
print(name)
hints: First, the variable 'name' stores the string "Python"
Then print() displays what's stored in 'name'
path.explanation:
To solve:
1. name = "Python" stores the text
2. print(name) displays: Python

print(x)

What is the value of y?
y = 5 + 3

path.content:

path.title: 🏆 Python Basics Complete!
path.content:
🏆 Congratulations! You've completed Python Basics!

✅ You've mastered:
• Python introduction
• Print statements
• Data types (Numbers, Strings, Booleans)
• Arithmetic operations
• String concatenation

🎉 You're ready for advanced topics!

💪 Keep coding and exploring!
